data.title: Walmart's Valuation Makes Zero Mathematical Sense
data.description:
I know Walmart Inc. (WMT) is a great company with a storied operating history. Don't get me wrong. But, my job as an investor and writer on Seeking Alpha is to find stocks that make analytical and financial sense to own, over both the short and long term.

With Walmart's valuation stats now passing both Costco (COST) and Amazon (AMZN) for new rarified heights, while sitting quite a distance above main business model rival Target (TGT), I find little to no reason to own the company. With earnings and free cash flow yields well "under" risk-free cash and money market rates, in the face of WMT's growth rates for these calculations projected at 10% or lower annually, why take on incredible equity price and economic recession risk in today's marketplace?

In all likelihood, investors paying over $100 a share will get burned in 2026-27. That's why I am taking a pass on WMT, and suggest math-based investors/shareholders contemplate the same. I have had a Strong Sell rating on Walmart since my last article in July here (with the price around $100). The minor rise since that effort hasn't changed my mind. If anything, the share price declines in competitors/peers Target, Costco, and Amazon since the summer have only served to jump the overvaluation spread at Walmart.

My view remains that the 2024-25 FOMO and bubble move in many mega-sized companies in America is doomed to bust (or at least cool for a number of years). My fear is Walmart could morph into a percentage loss leader in the overall blue-chip section of Wall Street next year. That will happen once investors care about valuation math again.

Crazy Overvaluation

The huge problem with buying Walmart at $115 (or even $100) is the stock has priced in many years of future business growth already. If we experience an oversized bear market on Wall Street and/or a recession in the U.S. economy during 2026, a "normalized" valuation move (reversion to the mean) could put the stock quote all the way back to $50-60 a share. Don't laugh. This is the "risk" side of the reward/risk equation for investors. Famed investors Warren Buffett and Charlie Munger have repeated over the decades, if you cannot handle a -50% drop in your equity portfolio (including individual names), you might want to put your capital to work elsewhere.

Let's review the significantly stretched valuation picture for Walmart. Perhaps the most eye-opening stats I can use to explain my point are found in the overall enterprise valuation on core EBITDA and sales. For a precedent, you have to travel back to the last technology boom peak of the year 2000, when Walmart was reaching for the zenith of its growth rate and investor enthusiasm for owning shares.

Taking the equity market capitalization plus net debt, EV to trailing EBITDA of 23x and sales of 1.4x is outrageously high. In comparison, WMT traded at an EV to EBITDA of 8x and sales of 0.5x in 2015. If your investment goal is to buy low and sell high, it's not rocket science to figure out what long-term investors should be doing today.

Further, on a variety of financial ratios comparing the share price to underlying business results, it's also clear to see Walmart is the most overvalued since at least 2002 (using a combination of data readouts). Price to trailing earnings (40.6x), sales (1.3x), cash flow (22.7x), and tangible book value (13.7x) can be argued as having even less value than 23 years ago because of WMT's dramatically larger size and unlikely odds to grow beyond macroeconomic rates in America following 2025 (law of large numbers concept).

I have been explaining the Walmart valuation problem for over a year to readers, with a stock quote that continues to ignore logic and reason. Perhaps my biggest issue over the last six months is Walmart's P/E is higher than a faster growing, better profit margin Amazon. It is also about to cross over the Costco P/E situation. When we review estimates for earnings going into early 2026, what am I getting for theoretical dollars in my pocket as an owner?

Honestly, Walmart is now the most expensive major retailer in America in December 2025. To me, this high valuation setup is simply not justified by a lower margin, lower growth operating business.

Examining its valuation as a function of profit margins and expected growth rates, Amazon is by far a smarter buy right now (and I don't particularly like Amazon either if a recession is approaching).

Another important issue for investors is Walmart's free cash flow [FCF] yield is rather minuscule at 1.64%. It's the lowest nominal yield since 2007 and the lowest "adjusted" yield to 1-year Treasury rates since 2008. If you can capture 1.9% extra in cash yield on investment from risk-free Treasuries, why not, as you can eliminate equity price risk in a recession scenario.

In comparison, both Costco and Target offer higher upfront FCF yields, while Amazon is reinvesting cash flow for stronger growth a year or two down the road (including higher-margin cloud AWS and all types of subscription services).

What about the dividend yield from WMT? Well, this stat is something of a fail vs. the S&P 500 index or the replicating SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). Walmart's trailing 0.79% yield is well under the S&P 500 equivalent rate of 1.05%, which has been rare over the past 20 years. This cash distribution is 0.26% less than the overall U.S. blue-chip equity average, and 2.8% less than a 1-year Treasury bill (not pictured).

Seeking Alpha's Quant Factor Valuation Grade is "F" for an overall score today, when parsing 19 valuation data sets. Reviewing Walmart's 5-year trading history and the current setup of alternative retailing companies, Walmart has less in concrete financial performance as a foundation for your investment. You are essentially overpaying to sleep better at night with the Walmart brand listed in your brokerage account. I just hope nightmares are not coming for your money in 2026.

Final Thoughts

What could prove me wrong and push Walmart even higher for price over the short term? Well, I am guessing more of the same as 2025 will be necessary. An economy staying out of recession matched with a stock market streaking ever higher would be my answer.

If you will, Walmart may be "priced for perfection." Nothing can go wrong in terms of business execution by management, or rising interest rates hurting investment valuation levels on Wall Street, or a recession pulling down retail sales and earnings.

My view is sooner or later the valuation problem will lead to slowing stock gains and eventually reverse them. That's the typical outcome for an overvalued darling late in the game during past bull/bear cycles.

Insiders at the company are not waiting to liquidate some of their holdings. Believe it or not, Walmart has been one of the most sold of any company during 2025 in America by management and leading shareholders (like the founding Walton family selling over $400 million in stock a few weeks ago). Below is a list of executive trading activity since the middle of October. It includes sales by nearly everyone in upper management.

You have to ask yourself if 40x earnings for a business growing sales by 5% and EPS by 10% is a sound long-term investment idea. I have been investing for nearly four decades, so my experience with similar situations says - absolutely not. Avoiding or selling Walmart should eventually prove the correct strategy (although nothing in life is guaranteed).

Where would I seriously consider purchasing a Walmart stake? For sure, a PEG ratio of 4x (40x P/E on 10% growth rate) is way out of bounds. In addition, a 2.5% or less earnings/FCF yield situation has no value to me with CPI inflation around 3% and short-term T-bills providing 3.5% in annual yield.

For a smart buy proposition, an upfront earnings/FCF yield of 5% would make better sense, alongside a reasonable PEG valuation closer to 2x (20x P/E on 10% growth rate). For a price target, assuming we hit current FY 2027 estimates of nearly $3, a 20-year median average P/E of 20x gets you to $60 per share for a normalized valuation. Such would be equal to a greater than -45% loss of wealth/capital, including the paltry dividend payout.

For truly bearish projections, a deep recession in 2026-27 could easily push earnings per share down to $2.50. A P/E of 20x would bring a price of only $50 per share. This is the simple valuation summary of why math-based investors should avoid/sell Walmart over $100.

If you had a legitimate argument that EPS growth rates were going to accelerate closer to 20% per annum, or Walmart was a takeover target in the days and weeks ahead, I might be willing to let today's extreme valuation slide. However, since neither variable will prove true in the end, the only rational conclusion for an investment outlook is the stock price is way too expensive when purchasing above $100.

Of course, an individual might have tax consequences to consider if wanting to liquidate the stock. Plus, I would definitely sell covered calls for extra income (and a hedge against price declines), assuming you want to continue holding a Walmart position. At some point, perhaps soon, the stock price/valuation will rerate back to a normal range. When this plays out (WMT's price could drop abruptly with little notice), your portfolio will be much happier avoiding any WMT exposure.

I continue to rate Walmart a Strong Sell until the valuation is reasonable again. It's entirely possible the quote will be the same or lower in 5-10 years, similar to what followed the 2000 price and valuation peak. If you want hard proof that U.S. stocks can trade at irrational levels, Walmart would be one of my exhibits in 2025, alongside Palantir (PLTR) and Strategy (MSTR) among numerous others.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: Canadian Natural Resources: Canada's 'Grand Bargain' Unlocks Major Long-Term Value
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I first covered Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), I highlighted their undervaluation despite their high-quality, long-life, low-decline assets and strong free cash flow sustaining a solid dividend while they’re improving their balance sheet.

CNQ continues to develop in a good direction, while a major deal in Canada paves the way for one of their key projects going forward, reinforcing the Strong Buy rating despite more weakness expected in the industry in the near future.

Internal Developments

Canadian Natural Resources reported an overall solid Q3 report, with 19% higher production (a record in both liquids and gas), thanks to the recent acquisitions and overall strong operational performance despite lower oil prices. The quarter also came with significant improvements in the operating cash flow, with C$1.82 billion in free cash flow in Q3 and C$6 billion over the first 9 months of 2025. Annualized, we’d probably look at about C$8 billion (roughly US$5.81 billion), which isn’t bad for a US$69 billion market cap given the weaker oil prices.

Still, keep in mind that following the recent AOSP swap with Shell (SHEL) that adds about 31,000 bbl/d of annual, zero-decline bitumen production, CNQ expects production between 1,560 MBOE/d and 1,580 MBOE/d and further improvements to ~1,620 MBOE/d in 2026 (at midpoint) on C$6.425 billion in CAPEX, with the management noting the following during the Q3 Earnings Release:

On November 1, we closed the AOSP swap with Shell Canada Limited. Canadian Natural now owns and operates 100% of the Albian oil sands mines and associated reserves and retains a non-operated 80% working interest in the Scotford Upgrader and Quest facilities (...), providing additional cash flow, driving long-term value creation for our shareholders. This swap also enhances our ability to integrate equipment and services across our mining operations, unlocking additional value through continuous improvement initiatives.

This just further reinforces the high-quality, long-life, and low-decline investment case, with further organic long-term potential from Jackpine Mine and IPEP + PFT expansions, while deals or commitments such as the one with Cheniere (LNG) help them gain exposure to better prices for their production and avoid the weaker local Canadian market.

This allows them to continue focusing on highly accretive acquisitions while maintaining a resilient asset base and even increase the annual dividend by ~12% and repay the debt. The dividend costs about $3.56 billion per year now, which should continue to be sustainable even if oil prices drop significantly, but I’d recommend being careful with what yield you’re expecting, as they are still an oil and gas company at the end of the day, and cash flows will vary because of it.

Their distribution policy was changed following the Chevron (CVX) assets acquisition, which I believe was a smart move overall, prioritizing a solid balance sheet before deploying their free cash flow into dividends no matter what.

With the net debt standing at C$17.067 billion as of the Q3 report, we’re still in the 60-40 payout (60% on shareholder returns, 40% for balance sheet improvements), soon likely getting into the 75-25 zone. This marks a 25-year consecutive dividend increase streak, with a massive ~21% CAGR since inception, by the way.

Financially, based on CNQ’s latest report, we generally see a limited amount of cash and equivalents, while the current assets don’t cover the current liabilities. Still, given the significant amounts of money this company can make even today and the massive amount of assets that more than offset their relatively low debt (that’s also low-cost), the overall financial health remains very solid.

Recently, CNQ also priced C$1.65 billion worth of notes with extremely good yields (sold to investors in Canada), with yields to maturity of 3.34%, ~3.80% and 4.59% for their 3-, 5-, and 10-year notes, which is very good in this market.

Macro Developments

To begin with a positive in this overall weak environment, we’ve seen TotalEnergies’ (TTE) release their annual energy outlook report recently, surprising the market with a massive change in expectations. TTE now expects oil demand to continue growing and peak a whole decade later than last year’s expectations given the fundamental shifts seen on a macro level, which should be an important thing to keep in mind despite the near-term weakness that’s expected.

The company highlights that this is a combination of the slower or even shifted green ambitions seen not only from companies, but even countries as a whole, combined with Trump’s expected “drill, baby, drill” policies in favor of oil and gas, on top of LNG licensing resumptions and green rollbacks in many countries, on top of coal plant installations in Asia and slower EV adoption globally.

Something else that gave the market some confidence was the fact that OPEC and its allies agreed to boost oil production in December while reportedly halting these hikes in Q1 2026 (at least). If that happens, we could potentially look at a boost in oil prices or at least something that can prevent them from falling even more since OPEC’s 2025 hikes played a key role in oil’s crash.

However, that’s most of the relatively good news when it comes to oil, while the market is more than likely going to focus on the things I’m mentioning below, as these are near-term factors with the potential to cause a lot of panic.

Probably the biggest potential can come from something that’s still a developing matter - the US-Venezuela situation. What began as an alleged war on drugs initiative has already degenerated significantly, with the US striking dozens of ships in the Caribbean, entering Venezuelan airspace, and even seizing a Venezuelan tanker recently - and already reportedly preparing to seize even more - leading to blockade fears that made oil stocks jump on that day.

Trump also sanctioned members of Maduro’s family and some ships he says move Venezuelan oil in the latest development of this situation. I’ll mention again that I see a potential attack on Venezuela as something that can cause massive disruptions not only in the oil market, but also significant issues in the region’s general trade with the US, as it can pose a risk for trading routes, cause delays, higher costs, etc. This would likely result in higher inflation for the American people, while a war so close to home can affect the market’s views and perceived risk, potentially helping the CAD gain some ground against the USD as well.

Another negative factor that caused oil prices to fall recently comes from the latest reports and expectations of rising US crude inventories and the even larger 2026 surplus. This negative sentiment was accentuated by pressure from reports such as JPMorgan’s, which talks about a potentially “overwhelming market oversupply” that can even cause oil prices to fall into the $30s by 2027.

Meanwhile, Trump’s sanctions on Russian oil combined with shaky steps towards peace in Ukraine can also pressure the price, as this war and the market’s fear were key factors behind oil basically doubling in a couple of months back ~4 years ago. Again, Russia will probably not go anywhere near their previous place in global trade any time soon, but this can still come with more pressure on the price of oil, especially due to the market’s expectations of a significantly negative factor easing.

We’ve also seen some good news from Canada recently in the form of what the market titled the “Grand Bargain” - with Canada and Alberta reaching a new partnership to lower emissions, unlock natural resources, and make the industry more competitive on a global scale. This comes as Alberta agreed to revamp its TIER system (i.e., their carbon pricing mechanism; increasing the minimum carbon price for large emitters to $130 per ton vs $95 now) to be more globally competitive and also support the $16.5 billion Pathways Alliance carbon capture project, of which CNQ is a leader.

Meanwhile, the state agreed to cancel/suspend the proposed emissions cap on oil and gas, which can be a significant shift for the country, removing a pretty big threat of basically forced production cuts unless they wanted to face fines. This also helps their Pathways Alliance project and offers some more certainty, basically securing the long-term viability of the oil sands, and it can also be perceived as the country realizing their importance for its economy - potentially leading to even more support going forward, especially if US pressure/tariffs/etc. continue.

This “energy security” initiative is also reinforced by the memorandum’s provision to explore a new bitumen pipeline to the West Coast, targeting ~1 million barrels per day to feed the Asian markets, helping Canadian companies secure better prices and helping them hedge against US uncertainty.

Risks Reiterated

Still, despite a couple of solid good developments for CNQ and the company’s solid business model, they remain tied to Canada and especially the oil and gas market, which will definitely be very volatile in the near future. Especially as the debt went up following the Chevron acquisition, there is potential to see this hurt the company, especially in a scenario with lower prices, which is certainly possible.

This can then affect their free cash flow and even the dividend, especially if they’re forced into a position where they have to choose between increasing the dividend to keep their streak or using the money to fund a very accretive acquisition. I’d always choose the latter, but the market may not react that well to any pressure on the dividend.

Valuation

To take into account the current oil prices, I’ll begin again with a free cash flow of US$5 billion, which is then expected to grow at a 4.5% CAGR over the first 5 years thanks to the potential coming from the Pathways project offsetting the higher carbon tax combined with upside potential from improving oil and especially gas prices (either AECO prices improve, which are terrible now, or they realize better ones through more international contracts), followed by a 3.5% CAGR over the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate.

I’ll go for an 8% WACC this time for a better margin of safety (above my estimated ~7.5% based on a roughly 8.6% cost of equity, 4.55% cost of debt), as well as US$12.4 billion in net debt and 2.09 billion shares outstanding.

This way, we get to an enterprise value of US$105.25 billion, an equity value of US$92.85 billion, and an intrinsic value of US$44.43 per share.

Conclusions

Overall, CNQ remains a strong buy, backed by very solid and improving financials, with a business model that’s excellent for long-term investors, with recent improvements reinforcing the thesis. There are risks, especially since they’re tied to Canada and their industry, but CNQ can definitely act as a well-run, high-quality way to diversify a portfolio’s exposure to these commodities.

data.title: The Fed, The 10-Year Yield, And The Income Investor's Nightmare
data.description:
Just recently, the Fed decided to cut the base rates precisely as the market had baked it in the cake. Even though, right after the cut was announced, we saw some positive system-wide reaction across the key market indices (QQQ) (SPY), the moves were not that pronounced, which again confirms that everything happened as expected.

Speaking of what the market had priced in going into 2026, it seems that also on this front there were no deviations from the base case. For example, looking at the consensus estimate, we can see that the probabilities are higher than 50% that already in April we will have yet another cut:

The interest rate cut probabilities for the earlier months remain below 50%, but certainly are material enough to warrant some interesting speculation (debate) for market participants.

The only thing that stood out from the general base case was the Fed's decision to embark on a short-term Treasury purchase program, which is commonly known as quantitative easing. However, if we peel back the onion a bit there, we will notice that actually a lot of this has to do with temporary balance sheet and market liquidity management amid the tax loss harvesting season. In other words, labeling this move as a major quantitative easing exercise would be too aggressive.

The long-term yield divergence

Both points that I outlined above - base rate cuts and short-term Government debt purchases - are inherently supportive for bringing the short-term yield down.

The thing is that these measures have nothing to do with the long-term yield department. And this is what we can see in the chart below:

The chart here shows that actually the delta between the short-term and long-term yields has widened despite the Fed's efforts to render the interest rate environment more accommodative for consumers and businesses.

What was quite surprising to me is that the driver for the spread-widening was not only the lower short-term yield but, to some extent, also the inflated U.S. 10-year Treasury.

I find it rather alarming that even with a) clearly accommodative monetary support package and b) lower and lower long-term Government debt issuances (less supply should help the pricing), the long-term yields are still going up.

Why 'higher-for-longer' in the 10-year is a problem for income investors

The elevated U.S. 10-year Treasury yield imposes problems for high-duration assets, which are securities or investments that derive their cash flows from long-term and stable sources (e.g., think of bond contracts, lease agreements, infrastructure usage, or renewable energy offtake agreements).

Investors who are inherently biased towards these types of investments are those who seek attractive income streams or passive cash flows for funding, say, retirement years.

For example, fixed-income products that have long-term tenures are likely to underperform in such an environment. In this category I would include leveraged fixed income CEF products like the ones from the PIMCO world: PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI), PIMCO Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO) and PIMCO Corporate and Income Opportunity Fund (PTY).

In the chart below, we can nicely see how PDI has declined despite having lower base rates in place, which, theoretically, is accommodative for most asset classes (just not the high-duration segment).

Real estate (VNQ) also falls into this category due to the presence of long-term leases and relatively stable growth elements. The same applies to infrastructure (BIP) and midstream (AMLP) products, which have both attracted long-term financing to match the long-term cash flow profile that is associated with their CapEx-intensive investments (e.g., midstream pipes that are operational for more than 20/30 years).

I would even include one of my favorite asset classes here - BDCs (BIZD). For the BDCs, which offer double-digit yields with certain pockets of opportunity for investors to lock in unfairly punished (and high-yielding) gems, the issue comes from two sides. Namely, they face serious pressures from the lower base rates, which directly reduce the effective private credit loan yields that are tied to SOFR. On top of that, since many BDCs rely on long-term unsecured note financing, the fact that the long-term yields remain elevated (or even have gone up) makes their life even more difficult - i.e., pressures from lower loan yields and higher cost of debt.

All of this means that the pressure on these high-duration asset cash flows, which is where many income investors are focused, will very likely increase if the trend does not get reversed. Namely, in this 'higher-for-longer' scenario, certain businesses will have to dedicate larger chunks of their cash flows towards debt service, which would, in turn, eat into the future growth potential. Now, if that comes in a single package with stubbornly high inflation, then the chances might be quite high that, in real terms, investors could experience negative passive income growth. For instance, for the REIT sector, where most REITs grow their FFO generation between 2% and 4%, that might be a real risk.

In a nutshell, I would not expect any kind of outperformance for REITs, MLPs, BDCs, infrastructure, and similar (high duration) asset classes.

However, in all of this, we can also find something positive, which is actually the fact that the asset prices here are unlikely to go up. This way the reinvestment yield risk gets nicely mitigated, where income investors can become less nervous about receiving less and less income from the reinvested or newly deployed capital.

So, in this context, the way that I position my capital is as follows:

  1. Underweight low-yielding REITs and long-term fixed income instruments, which either do not grow at all or grow at 2%–4% per year (to reduce risk of negative real income growth).
  2. Overweight high-yielding durable income plays even if their prospects for strong total returns are weak due to the struggling price component from elevated long-term yields and high duration factor. For example, selected MLPs, BDCs, and the same PIMCO products that I outlined above fit well in this category, as their fundamentals are generally robust and yields are high enough to provide ample room for reinvestments (at least partial), which would more than offset the headwinds from more expensive long-term borrowing.

data.title: Opendoor: The Right Path But A Tough One
data.description:
Opendoor (OPEN) is a popular stock that gives digital exposure to the real estate industry. With a new CEO's bold strategy, this will likely help their business, but I remain cautious about the state of the balance sheet he inherited and await more stabilization.

Summary of Previous Theses

OPEN is a stock I've covered twice, first in July at $2 per share and again in September under $9. I rated it a hold both times, as I had doubts about the business model: a principal (not an agent) in real estate.

Compared to a rival like Zillow (Z), which operates a platform and takes a fee, Opendoor purchases real estate from sellers and then finds a buyer. The value-add is providing liquidity and the cost efficiencies of a digital platform, but the history of gross margins (seen above) shows how volatile this can be.

Opendoor is essentially a house flipper. Where individuals who flip are selective in their acquisitions, Opendoor's raison d'être somewhat precluded selection, exposing them to risky acquisitions that are difficult to sell profitably, particularly if rates go up.

While they announced plans in Q2 earnings to work more with real estate agents, I worried that commissions would compete with the spread Opendoor must earn as principal.

I surmised both times that rate cuts could provide near-term gains in earnings as they are able to sell inventory at higher prices but wasn't sure that they could do this consistently, without external catalysts. I wrote:

Otherwise, Opendoor really needs to get its overhead down to produce an operating profit, whatever happens with interest rates and agents. Much of their talk about AI in Q2 was on training for the purposes of price modeling and assistant software for their agents. Less emphasis was on cost reduction.

For that reason, I considered them fairly valued for favorable outcomes but not priced for setbacks.

Q3 2025 Updates And More

Since my last coverage, Q3 2025 results have been released, and the Federal Reserve has declared three rate cuts of 25 BPS each. The Q3 earnings call was also the first of newly-hired CEO Kasra "Kaz" Nejatian, and he was clear that he would have different priorities going forward, so there's a lot to review.

Q3 revenue was down 34% YoY, primarily due to a decline in volume. 2,568 homes were sold, compared to 3,615 in the prior year. In their Form 10Q, management observed:

In the third quarter of 2025, U.S. housing market activity remained subdued amid elevated mortgage rates and persistent affordability constraints.

The price history of the 10Y Note (US10Y) shows this trend, as it directly influences the rates that lenders set for mortgages. Throughout 2025, rates remained at 4% or higher.

Without positive tailwinds, this has resulted in continued losses, both for the quarter and the nine months ended September.

As a result, about $200M in cash was raised through the issuance of new shares, late in the quarter.

Overall, 2025 has led to a shrinking of the balance sheet. From the end of 2024 to the end of Q3 2025, the impacts were the following:

Assets

  • Cash: $671M to $962M
  • Real estate inventory: $2.16B to $1.05B
  • Total assets: $3.1B to $2.7B.

Liabilities

  • Current portion of debt: $432M to $813M
  • Long-term portion of debt: $1.87B to $966M
  • Total liabilities: $2.4B to $1.9B

It shows a more conservative posture in response to this more depressed market, with a net reduction in inventory, growth and retention of cash, and reduction in total debt.

While we must wait until Q4 results before there are financial results under the new CEO, one of his first moves in this time has been a dividend warrant for OPEN shares of Series K, Series A, and Series Z issues (spelling Nejatian's name). Shareholders (and convertible note holders as adjusted) received 1 of each for every 30 shares of OPEN. Detailed in the latest Form 10Q, they have a term of one year and the following exercise prices:

  • Series K: $9
  • Series A: $13
  • Series Z: $17

These are an interesting statement by the new CEO, reflecting his belief that OPEN shares should become more valuable over time, in spite of the weak financials that preceded his arrival.

Lastly, the Federal Reserve, after no cuts or hikes for most of the year, finally did their first 25 BPS rate cut at the September FOMC meeting, with two more at the following meetings. The most recent was just this month, for 75 BPS cuts overall. As these were primarily effective in Q4, we'll have to wait to see impacts from these as well.

Outlook

While many were likely watching OPEN for potential catalysts from rate cuts, I believe Nejatian's role as CEO and his strategic priorities for the company will be much more important. I'll summarize his points from the earnings call that stood out to me:

  • Overhead/cost reduction
  • Utilization of software and AI to drive volume
  • Opposition to shareholder dilution

Let's start by focusing on the first. In the call, he made the following critique of the prior operations:

I was genuinely shocked when I found out that one of Opendoor's biggest expenses in the first half of this year was millions of dollars paid to a well-known consulting firm to tell Opendoor how to do its job. Everywhere I looked in my first 30 days, I found consultants making decisions that should have been made by executives.

He mentioned that headcount had been reduced from about 1,400 to 1,100 already. He also hinted that cost and time could be reduced by successful implementation of AI in regular operations:

We believe machines are better at pricing assets than humans. We believe AI will empower us to avoid toilful work, and we can have a leaner and more aggressive company.

The headcount reduction aligns with this somewhat. It also feeds into his thoughts on increasing volume to produce viable earnings. While this means more risk-bearing, it is simply true that Opendoor needs to scale up in order to make money:

To use Wall Street terms, Opendoor is going to be kind of like a market maker in the future, not a prop desk. We're going to profit from flow, speed and tight spreads, not on bets on the direction of the economy.

The quote above directly reflects my previous concerns about the company. A principal needs good volume at a reasonable spread to make money, and Opendoor had never managed to do that. To support this, he spoke to rebuilding the tech behind their inspection process, allowing for better AI that can price effectively.

Once again, some progress on this front may already have been made. Their Q3 earnings release noted:

To give you a sense of our progress, for the week ending September 15th we entered into contract to buy 120 homes; in the last week of October we entered into contract to buy 230 homes.

It also spoke to concentrating on specific real estate markets, where they can set trends and create a "flywheel" effect, as opposed to creating scale through expansion into several markets simultaneously.

I largely agree with the wisdom of these efforts. It's much easier to utilize and train effective AI with assets from common markets, as well as to compete effectively with limited capital, particularly if they focus on markets where the biggest opportunity for churn exists.

The old model was like trying to make a market for buyers and sellers of different stocks. This shift brings their model closer to one of a market maker that successfully plays middleman for buyers and sellers of the same assets.

Another important factor here was the de-emphasis on the agent-friendly strategy that old management mentioned in the Q2 earnings call, instead focusing on D2C transactions. Again, I was concerned that agent commissions would hurt their ability to achieve a spread. Moreover, an agent is another actor in the transaction, another person to have on board when what they need is volume.

Lastly, on financing, Nejatian said:

Let me be clear, I despise dilution. If we issue a share, it has only one job, to make every other share worth more for our existing shareholders. But to give us some breathing room, I made a decision in my first week to use our existing ATM program to raise nearly $200 million. This bought us the time we needed to deal with the notes without a gun to our head.

This was likely the correct decision in creating stability on the balance sheet. I am inclined to think he will not dilute if not necessary going forward, but a lot of that will depend on cash needs going forward.

Valuation

OPEN's price is recently around $6.50 per share, for a market cap around $6.5B. I see it as a test: Can OPEN get to $650M in earnings in at least a few years, to justify a future P/E of 10?

Overhead has always exceeded gross profit. Efforts to get this down are the key to future profits. If this can be done, while increasing volume to levels from a couple of years ago, there's a possibility for at least a couple hundred million in earnings. TTM interest expense is currently $135M too, so any reduction here would boost earnings as well.

There is a path to earnings growth paved before us that was not present under old management in the summer. Nejatian is correct to state that it's pointless to freeze up over macro-focused risk aversion, but the company still needs favorable conditions in their markets for a smart plan to work.

Any setbacks, anything that discourages new homeowners from buying, slows down this plan, and the company may have to issue more equity to stabilize the balance sheet and provide sufficient capital.

My view is slightly more positive, but as I see just as much room for dilution ahead of a Q4 balance sheet, I'm inclined to maintain my Hold rating for now. A tighter entry and appropriately sized entry, under $3.50, seem better managed for risk at the current moment.

Conclusion

The new CEO seems to understand the strategic situation for Opendoor better than previous management. His ambitious strategy may be the only thing that can save the company, but his late arrival means the company has a weak balance sheet that needs improvement first. Despite my reassurance in the strategy, I find it prudent to maintain my Hold rating.

data.title: Fidus Investment: 9% Yield Not Worth The Risk
data.description:
My Hold Thesis For Fidus Investment (FDUS)

Fidus Investment is an externally-managed BDC with a record of originating new loans and thereby consistently growing its debt investments. Fidus Investment has a large pool of first-lien investments from which it derives interest income, has a low PIK percentage, but a high cost-based non-accrual ratio.

All of these metrics considered, the BDC is not the most secure dividend payer, in my opinion, and though you might find the 9% yield appealing, Fidus Investment may cut its dividend in 2026.

Fidus Investment’s Portfolio Structure And Other Thoughts

The BDC’s portfolio now has a portfolio value of $1.2 billion, and 82% of Fidus Investment’s assets are secured debt investments, meaning either first-liens or second-liens. The total first-lien percentage is 73% (cost) and 72% using the fair value benchmark.

Fidus Investment’s portfolio, over time, has shifted into higher-quality first-liens meaning the BDC has progressively lowered its risks. Previously (before 2021), Fidus Investment was predominantly invested in lower-ranked, second-liens which had higher yields, but also higher risks.

Fidus Investment is growing by making new loans. Just like banks, Fidus Investments uses available funds to make loans to middle market companies and collects interest income from the borrower. Since BDCs also receive loan repayments, both scheduled and unscheduled, what matters most to Fidus Investment is how quickly the portfolio is growing on a net basis, that is, after consideration of loan repayments.

In Q325 Fidus Investment increased its investments, on a net basis, by $37.8 million, which was the second quarter in a year in which the BDC managed to grow in excess of its loan repayments.

As of September 30, 2025, the investment portfolio's fair value totaled approximately $1.2 billion, consisting of investments across 92 active portfolio companies. The portfolio is diversified across various industries, including Information Technology Services, Business Services, Healthcare Products/Services, and Niche Manufacturing.

Fidus Investment had a 72% FV first-lien percentage in its portfolio, which is neither good nor bad. This first-lien percentage compares against a 58% percentage for FS KKR Capital (FSK) which I urged readers to avoid. Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD) and Oaktree Specialty Lending (OCSL) have higher first-lien percentages of 97% and 83%.

Fidus Investment had a cost-based non-accrual ratio of 2.8% (Q325) which makes FDUS the BDC with the second-lowest non-accrual ratio, after Goldman Sachs BDC. Both Oaktree Specialty Lending and FS KKR Capital have significantly higher non-accrual ratios, which makes them high-risk investments and not suitable for you if dividend safety is your primary concern.

Fidus Investment is externally-managed with means the company is paying fees to an external manager. The BDC has seen an increase in both base management fees and interest expenses in Q325 which curtailed its net investment income before and after taxes.

Fidus Investment’s loans generated $17.4 million in NII in Q325, down 19% YoY. Despite a portfolio that is growing in value, Fidus Investment’s higher costs associated with managing the loan portfolio, have limited its NII potential in the last year.

This creates trickle-down problems for the BDC as Fidus Investment does not have the best spread between GAAP NII and distribution, which is also commonly expressed as excess coverage.

In Q325 Fidus Investment fell $0.08 short of its total dividend, which was comprised of a $0.43 base dividend and a $0.14 supplemental dividend. The BDC, however, consistently overcovered its $0.43 share base distribution since Q124.

In the last seven quarters, Fidus Investment undercovered its dividend six times, which could make the BDC a primary contender for a dividend cut in 2026.

Of course, BDCs have spillover income, which is the sum total of undistributed taxable income that was earned in previous quarters and that acts as a reserve pool to make up shortfalls in dividend payments. In Q325, this undistributed taxable income amounted to $39.5 million, or approximately $1.09 if broken down to the per-share level.

Fidus Investment’s spillover income is therefore equal to 2.5 payments of its $0.43 base dividend. Fidus Investment has paid supplemental dividends that have varied widely from quarter to quarter. With the spread/excess coverage trend looking the way it does, I expect Fidus Investment to cease making supplemental dividends in 2026 and fall back on paying only its base dividend of $0.43 every quarter.

Since Fidus Investment has paid supplemental dividends regularly in the last seven quarters, the suspension of supplemental dividends effectively equals a dividend cut.

Fidus Investment Is A Hold, Not A Buy

The price-to-book ratio for Fidus Investment is 1.03 which sets the BDC apart from the likes of Goldman Sachs BDC, Oaktree Specialty Lending, and FS KKR Capital, all of which battle with above-average non-accruals and which have negative spread pictures.

Goldman Sachs BDC, Oaktree Specialty Lending and FS KKR Capital all have price-to-book ratios well below 1.0 which shows market concerns about their expected dividend payments in the future. While Fidus Investment is priced above book value, I would still be very hesitant to invest in this BDC, given the trend in coverage.

With few exceptions, I am not buying BDCs unless I get a discount to BV, for safety reasons. I pay approximately 0.9 book value for Fidus Investment, given its high cost-based non-accrual ratio and weak trend in its spread between GAAP NII and distribution.

Fidus Investment’s BVPS was $19.56 in Q325 and the market currently assigns a 3% premium to this book value. This means I’d buy FDUS at a price of $17.50 or below, at which point I would transition from a hold to a buy rating.

With the coverage trend looking poor and income compressed from higher expenses, my rating for Fidus Investment is, for the time being, only hold.

Suitability, Risk Analysis

High Floating-Range Percentage: 72% of Fidus Investments’ loans are floating-rate. This means that interest rate cuts are going to be a major risk for the BDC in the next twelve months.

Exposure to First-Liens: A large percentage of first-liens means the BDC may not take sufficient advantage of higher-yielding, but lower-ranking debt investments. This means they forgo yield, which could suppress potential for income and BVPS growth.

My Verdict: Hold

Fidus Investment is neither a great nor a bad pick in a crowded field of BDCs that range from subpar BDCs like Prospect Capital to consistently stellar performers like Ares Capital.

Fidus Investment is a solid investment in the sense that it covers its base dividend, but its supplemental dividends are not covered by GAAP NII, which casts a cloud over the BDC’s ability to keep paying them in 2026 when the Fed is expected to lower interest rates and thereby exacerbate current dividend shortfalls.

Fidus Investment is selling at a price above book value, which for me doesn’t make the BDC a viable option. I would buy Fidus Investment at a discount to book value, and I would be eager to buy below $17.50 if the share price ever gets there.

data.title: IonQ: Nvidia Of Quantum Still Volatile, Highly Dilutive, And Expensive
data.description:
I previously covered IonQ, Inc. (NYSE:IONQ) in September 2025, discussing how its intensified R&D efforts had paid off handsomely, as observed in the expanding partnerships across numerous leading quantum/AI institutes and the double-digit revenue growth.

Despite their ability to rapidly expand the quantum ecosystem through accretive acquisitions, I had believed that the stock might have exhausted much of its upward momentum despite the elevated volumes, resulting in my Hold rating then.

In this article, I shall discuss why IONQ remains a Hold here, with the nascency of its high-growth stages already triggering its mixed investment thesis across the aggressive acquisitions/in-house R&D efforts and the dilutive capital raises/expensive valuations.

Combined with the uncertain path to breakeven and the elevated short interest ratio, I am of the opinion that it may be more prudent to observe from the sidelines in the meantime.

IONQ Boasts Robust Quantum Prospects

IONQ 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Hold rating, IONQ has continued to execute according to its historical playbook across intensified R&D/innovation efforts and accretive acquisitions, with the latest Oxford Ionics addition already improving its investment thesis.

The acquisition brings together two world record holders for highest quantum fidelity across IONQ's proprietary Electronic Qubit Control technology [EQC] with two-qubit gate performance at fidelity exceeding 99.99% in October 2025 and Oxford Ionics' prior record at 99.97% in 2024.

With IONQ set to demonstrate the integrated IonQ/Oxford Ionics 256-qubit system with 99.99% 2-qubit gate fidelity in 2026, I am of the opinion that the quantum company may deliver an accelerated commercial/enterprise/federal monetization cadence ahead.

This is because the 99.99% fidelity performance has already realized the notably improved accuracy and reliability rates while unlocking "more complex algorithms that simply will never be tackled by lower-performance systems."

Most importantly, readers may want to note that IONQ's 256-qubit system already matches "about 10 to the power of 20 H100 NVIDIA GPUs" in terms of horsepower, while consuming a notably lower power consumption.

This development presents its promising potential of delivering a system "more powerful than the largest supercomputer humanity will ever build, even assuming hundreds or thousands of years of classical technology progress," with further progress expected as they reiterate their 2030 ambition of 2M qubit systems in the recent Investor Day in September 2025.

This is especially since IONQ has made the strategic choice to leverage the existing semiconductor foundries instead of relying on new, untested technologies, allowing them to scale rapidly while directly integrating into existing data centers.

This is on top of their use of the mature 128nm node instead of cutting edge 5nm/2nm offerings, allowing them to offer readily available quantum computing offerings at notably cheaper prices.

The excellent "technology readiness" strategy may also be why IONQ has been able to rapidly monetize its quantum offerings with FQ3'25 revenues of $39.86M (+92.6% QoQ/+221.4% YoY) while twice raising the FY2025 revenues to $108M at the midpoint (+150.7% YoY).

The quantum company's robust monetization is significantly aided by its ability to service the research and the enterprise/commercial end markets along with the federal segment, as observed in recent deals with:

  • CERN, Rolex SA, Swiss government, and academic institutions to "support research, collaboration, and quantum tech awareness,"
  • University of Chicago "to accelerate the development of novel quantum technology by University of Chicago researchers,"
  • Einride AB to drive "optimization of shipment allocation" and deliver "greater cost-effectiveness" in its commercial transport solutions,
  • Centre for Commercialization of Regenerative Medicine [CCRM] to "accelerate next-generation therapeutic development using hybrid quantum and quantum-AI technologies,"
  • U.S. Department of Energy to "advance the development and deployment of quantum technologies in space," and
  • Heven AeroTech for the "deployment of quantum technologies" in real-world autonomous aerial systems/drones

With it underscoring the versatility of its quantum computing offerings and the immense TAM of its future monetization opportunities, which perhaps lend credence to the management's claim of IONQ being the "NVIDIA Of The Quantum Space."

This is significantly aided by IONQ advancing to stage B of the United States government’s Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency [DARPA] Quantum Benchmarking Initiative [QBI], with it marking its ability to deliver a "useful quantum computer.”

Valuation Risks Attributed To High-Growth Startup Stage

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why IONQ has felt confident to hint at their "ability to now pursue potentially 3-digit million-dollar opportunities" across the US government's Golden Dome initiative and the growing diversification to international opportunities, beyond the existing government contracts worth over $100M and the current 30% of its revenues, respectively.

This is aided by the aggressive acquisition strategy/investments in their go-to-market strategy and software capabilities beyond their existing infrastructure sales/QCaaS offerings through cloud providers, allowing them to drive expanded monetization opportunities moving forward.

As a result of the robust top-line growth prospects and the consensus promising forward estimates at a CAGR of +86.2% through FY2030, I urge readers to look beyond IONQ's likely to be prolonged cash burn, based on the management's FY2025 adj EBITDA margin guidance of -195.3% (+53.5 points YoY).

This is especially since the quantum company has been capitalizing on the elevated stock prices/capital raises to deliver an increasingly rich balance sheet with a net cash position of $3.5B as of October 14, 2025.

IONQ Valuations

Otherwise, IONQ is likely suitable only for those with a higher risk appetite, attributed to the expensive FWD EV/Sales valuation of 159.15x by the time of writing before eventually moderating to my estimated FY2030 EV/Sales valuations of 9.76x, based on the Enterprise Value of $17.58B and the consensus FY2030 revenue estimates of $1.8B.

When comparing it to its quantum peers with commercial/federal monetization opportunities, including:

  • QBTS at current FWD EV/Sales of 337.65x and estimated FY2030 EV/Sales valuations of 14.28x
  • International Business Machines Corporation (IBM) at 5.13x/4.04x, and
  • The privately held IQM Quantum Computers at $1B in Enterprise Value

It is apparent that IONQ's investment thesis remains in a nascent stage, with the stock likely to remain expensive before growing into its premium valuations as it increasingly monetizes its growing research/enterprise/federal opportunities.

IONQ's Breakout Overly Done - Maintain Hold

IONQ 4Y Stock Price

For now, IONQ has remained well supported at its uptrend support line, with the stock seemingly following a trading pattern of breakout and then sideways trading as observed between the September 2024 bottom and the second breakout in September 2025.

IONQ Technical Indicators

A similar trend has been observed in IONQ's technical indicators as well, with the reasonable RSIs and the moderating trading volumes implying a bullish support at current levels.

Does this mean that the stock is likely to sustain its upward momentum moving forward and thus warrant an upgraded rating to a Buy?

Not quite.

My estimated FY2030 EV/Sales valuations of 9.76x assume that IONQ is going to continue trading sideways between the 52-week high of $84s and the November 2025 bottom of $41s - an unlikely outcome, given the established uptrend support line since the September 2024 bottom.

At the same time, I am of the opinion that the quantum computing company is likely to continue diluting shareholders' equity moving forward, with the management's growth playbook already triggering the ongoing shares outstanding expansion to 294.52M as of FQ3'25 (+37.4% YoY).

Combined with its high-growth start-up stage, it goes without saying that IONQ is only suitable for those with a long-term investing trajectory, since break-even is unlikely to occur within this decade, as observed in the consensus forward estimates.

Given the mixed investment thesis and the volatility risks arising from the elevated short interest ratio of 16.3%, I prefer to observe from the sidelines and maintain my Hold rating here.

data.title: A Wave Of Good News For My Largest Holdings
data.description:
You win some, you lose some.

Q3 has been rather disappointing for me, with recession fears hurting companies like Caesars (CZR), and then Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) surprising all of us with a dividend cut.

But I am finally getting some good news from my other portfolio holdings. I will discuss those in today's article, along with updates on many of my top holdings.

First, my largest international holding, Patria Investments (PAX), just shared a surprise of its own: it is going to hike its dividend by 8.3% going into 2026, marking the start of a new dividend policy. I predicted this shift in policy earlier this year, expecting it to send a strong signal and push the share price to higher levels, and that's exactly what has happened. The market reacted to this by sending the share price nearly 10% higher on the day of the announcement, and that's after already rising by 50% earlier this year:

Its Q3 results were very strong, with continued rapid growth in its AUM, which is resulting in growing fee income. The company now expects to exceed the high end of its fundraising target for 2025, and expressed growing confidence in reaching its targets for 2026 and 2027.

So far, they have consistently underpromised and overdelivered, and if they can meet these targets, we would expect a lot more upside and dividend growth in the years ahead.

That's very welcome, especially coming from such a large holding, representing 11% of our International Portfolio at High Yield Landlord.

Even better news, our second-largest international holding, Helios Towers (HTWS / OTCPK:HTWSF) surged by 13% in a single day after announcing much better results than anticipated, hitting many 2026 targets essentially a year early, which will now allow it to now enter its next strategic phase called "IMPACT 2030", aiming at continuing its rapid organic growth, all while returning more than $400 million to shareholders via buybacks and dividends by 2030.

That's about 20% of its market cap!

Its stock has now more than doubled since the beginning of this year. A huge win for us, given that it is a very big position, representing 10% of our International Portfolio.

Another piece of good news is that Whitestone REIT (WSR) just recently received yet another buyout offer, which initially sent its share price soaring by 15%.

Whitestone's third biggest shareholder, owning 9.2% of the company, is a private equity firm called MCB Real Estate. Last year, it offered to buy out Whitestone at $15 per share, but this deal was rejected as it would undervalue the company's NAV, which we estimate to be closer to $20.

Now, they are back with a $15.20 offer, and some threats of potential activism if they reject the offer once again.

But I expect the offer to get rejected again, and would not be surprised if we see yet another (higher) offer within a year from now. Whitestone's properties are enjoying solid growth, even as cap rates are expected to compress as a result of the undersupply, improving fundamentals, and rate cuts.

MCB knows this, and this is why they want to buy out the whole company. But Whitesone knows this as well, and that is why they refuse to sell at a steep discount.

I side with Whitestone's management here, but appreciate MCB's efforts to put additional pressure on Whitestone to unlock value for shareholders.

It is great news overall, and I would expect the REIT's share price to continue rising closer to its NAV. There is a lot of upside potential left.

International Workplace Group (IWG / OTCPK:IWGFF), our single largest holding by far, reported a strong acceleration in growth, with fee income from its managed space division rising by 83% year-over-year.

They also reported a 40% surge in signings following the expansion of their sales team, which translated into 335 new signings in a single quarter! That's huge, considering that they only operate about 4,000 locations right now.

So the capital-light transformation of their business is happening at a very fast pace, and this is finally starting to be rewarded. Its stock initially rose by 3.5% and it is now up 55% from its lows earlier this year:

Kite Realty Group (KRG), another top 5 position for us, also announced a 7.4% dividend hike, which is the biggest hike that I know of for any REIT trading at such a low multiple.

Its larger peer, Kimco Realty (KIM), also hiked its dividend by 4%. It probably could have hiked it even more, but it also launched a new $750 million share buyback plan and likely wanted to retain more cash flow for this.

Uniti Group (UNIT) also initially surged 7% after reporting strong Q3 results and closing its merger with Winstream, which marks an important turning point in the company's history. It removes the existential tenant risk that depressed the company's market sentiment and held back its growth. It also now positions the company as the premier insurgent fiber provider with attractive long-term growth prospects, supported by the AI revolution as well.

Here is what they said:

"There has never been a better time to be a wholesale fiber provider. Broadband trends are accelerating across virtually all categories, especially AI-driven use cases, as evidenced by another strong quarter of new bookings. Although we're building substantial amounts of new fiber, especially for the hyperscalers, our scaled national footprint gives us terrific lease-up potential, driving our blended cash yields to 34%, the highest we've ever seen, and we believe there's more to come."

As they now keep growing and reinforcing their balance sheet, we also expect their average interest rates to come down substantially, given that it is currently at 8%. Just recently, they issued some debt at a 5.7% interest rate, so there is a lot of potential for growth just from its debt costs coming back down now that its tenant issue has been resolved.

More upside ahead:

Finally, quite a few of our other holdings also just reported strong results, but have missed out on the upside so far due to the broader REIT market weakness.

All the following REITs beat expectations in at least some respect and/or hiked their full-year guidances:

  • NNN REIT (NNN)
  • W.P. Carey (WPC)
  • CubeSmart (CUBE)
  • Camden Property Trust (CPT)
  • Sila Realty Trust (SILA)
  • Armada Hoffler (AHH)
  • Macerich (MAC)
  • BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA)

All of this just to say that Q3 ended up being stronger than we initially feared.

The other important news is that the Fed just cut interest rates for the third time this year.

Moreover, the debt market now expects interest rates to be cut by another 50-75 basis points within the next year, bringing interest rates to just 2.75-3%:

The reason why rates are being cut is simply that the labor market is rapidly weakening, which could spell trouble for the broader economy.

I think that AI has a lot to do with the latest layoff announcements, and as this becomes increasingly clear ot the market, more investors will also recognize just how strong a deflationary force AI is going to be.

This should then pull long-term interest rates to lower levels as well, benefiting REITs. But even ignoring that, simply getting lower short-term rates should strongly benefit REIT valuations over the coming years.

That's because it will influence capital flows, and right now, there is a record-breaking $7 trillion of capital in money-market funds:

As interest rates are lowered, I expect some of this capital to gradually make its way back to higher-yielding options, including REITs, which will then push their share prices to higher levels.

It is the same reason why REITs crashed as short-term rates were hiked, but in reverse.

This correlation between REITs and short-term rates, of course, isn't perfect, but over time, there has been a clear inverse correlation, with REITs rising as rates are cut, and vice versa.

REITs are already up about 38% on average since rate cuts began in late 2023, and they have a lot more to give, given that REIT valuations are still at a near-decade low relative to the broader stock market (SPY).

And even if you don't buy into the upside potential from rate cuts, you should still take some comfort in knowing that valuations are historically low, which, at the minimum, should provide a margin of safety in today's highly uncertain environment.

Tech stocks are hot right now, but their extreme valuations make them speculative. REITs are less exciting and not getting much attention, but their businesses are far more resilient, owning cash-flowing real assets that are essential to our society, and trading at large discounts.

Similarly, REITs were discounted and overlooked heading into the dotcom crash, but they strongly recovered and were highly rewarding in the aftermath:

Could something similar play out over the coming years?

Only time will tell, but I sleep better at night knowing that my portfolio is not just betting on the potential bubble continuing to inflate, even as valuations seem stretched.

With REITs, we are investing in resilient real assets that generate substantial cash flow and are priced conservatively. As interest rates continue to be cut, this should attract more investors, eventually resulting in higher valuations.

So despite the recent setbacks in Alexandria, Caesars, and others, we remain confident in our long-term thesis and will keep on accumulating more shares at discounted REITs at High Yield Landlord, predicting more good news ahead.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2026: The Revenge Of The S&P 493 (Market Outlook)
data.description:
Family Pictures Of The General Market

Christmas time is usually a perfect occasion to shoot some family photos. If the S&P 500 (SPY) were a large family, this year we would probably see seven people standing in the middle, smiling, while the other 493 are sitting in the background with far fewer smiles. Actually, for over two years, Mr. (or Grandpa) Market has simply considered this to be normal. As 2026 approaches, should we expect next year's family picture will be the same, or could we expect more relatives to join the party?

In this article, I want to share my 2026 market outlook, basing my reasoning on the divergence that we have all witnessed in the market between the top 7 stocks and the remaining 493. I will argue that 2026 might be the year when the divergence starts to be reduced, as if the photographer finally asked everyone to shuffle in and smile.

This article won't be bearish on the Magnificent 7. After all, I have recently argued that Alphabet has won (GOOG; GOOGL) and that Amazon (AMZN) is the other winner of the AI-race. However, in this article I want to show how 2026 should be the year when the rest of the market awakens and starts contributing thanks to AI.

Three Considerations On The Economy

Before we look more in-depth at the S&P 500 and end up with a 2026 price target, allow me to briefly share three views I have on next year's economy. They will be useful to build the correct frame for the rest of the article.

1. The U.S. Will Not Enter Into A Recession

First of all, from the data we are seeing, there is no reason to expect that the U.S. will go through a recession next year. Yes, consumer spending is a bit cautious, but the country's GDP will continuously be supported by large public and private investments, driven mainly by the AI buildout. Moreover, defense spending is also increasing, further boosting the country's GDP, which is expected to grow at around 2%.

2. Crude Prices Will Decline

Although we have been talking about an energy demand boom, crude prices are likely to move down to below $55 a barrel. This is because supply is high due to increasing production. While this may not help oil stocks, it will surely be a deflationary force that will play its role in keeping inflation between 2% and 3%, even though tariffs will impact the whole year. So, energy prices will be a likely tailwind for many companies' margins.

3. Interest Rates Will Be (A Bit) Lower

The December cut is finally real, but we also received more convincing news that the Fed may be done cutting rates, unless the data on the economy changes to a big extent. This means that we can expect one or two cuts in 2026, but that we have to get used to an environment where interest rates stay around 3.50% for longer than expected. This has two implications. The easier one is that the U.S. dollar might weaken slightly through the year. The second one is that higher interest rates will work their way into valuation models that so far have considered them just as temporary. As a result, we could see an equity rerating cycle.

Macroeconomic Pillars

Let's start with one piece of evidence that this article will try to challenge. According to JP Morgan,

S&P 500 earnings are expected to grow 13% in 2026. Magnificent 7 earnings growth may decelerate slightly to 20%, while the rest of the index is expected to grow 11%, contributing 64% of overall profit growth. However, this “broadening out” has thus far failed to materialize, and 2026 Mag 7 earnings estimates have been revised up by 3.4% vs. -1.2% for the S&P 493 since the start of the year.

So, right now, analysts are expecting the Mag 7 to report 20% EPS growth in 2026, while the remaining 493 see their earnings estimates drop by 1.2% to 11.3%. In other words, while many expect earnings growth to broaden, reality seems distant from this take.

Analysts expect higher earnings growth for the Mag 7, especially from Q2 2026 onward. At the same time, many believe that the remaining 493 companies will see aggregate double-digit growth starting in Q1 2026. If we put together the Mag 7 and the other 493, we can immediately see the growth rates that analysts are expecting quarter by quarter:

Q4 2025 Q1 2026 Q2 2026 Q3 2026 FY 2026

Mag 7 EPS growth

 14.9% 14.9% 17.5% 19.7% 20.0%

Other 493 EPS growth

 5.0% 11.0% 11.4% 14.6% 11.3%

S&P500 rev. growth

 8.0% 7.1% 6.3% 7.1% 7.1%
S&P500 EPS growth 12.2% 13.6% 13.6% 17.5% 14.5%

As we enter Phase 2 of the AI revolution, we will continue seeing massive infrastructure projects, but we should also see the positive impact of AI on enterprise efficiency. Something seems to be happening. FactSet, for example, shows that companies citing "AI" on their Q3 earnings calls have, on average, seen greater price changes this year. Two years ago, we could have linked this to a general AI hype. But investors are starting to demand real numbers, and it seems as if companies that are implementing AI manage to report better numbers that drive prices higher.

Now, I want to show six key macroeconomic data points, which I will call pillars, that could turn 2026 into the year of the long-awaited reconciliation. I am not expecting that by year end, the rest of the market will grow as fast as the Mag 7, which I still expect to lead the market. But I do see some factors that could play in favor of gap-narrowing dynamics.

This is my thesis: I think 2026 will be a year where the 493 stocks that have been left behind could close a bit of the gap with the Mag 7. In this case, the S& P500 will finish the year higher. We will see at the end what my target is.

Pillar 1: Yields

First of all, as Warren Buffett and many other investors teach, interest rates are the force of gravity that pulls down equity valuations. By now, we should accept that a zero-interest environment is behind us with no chance of reappearing soon. If so, some investors simply decided to consider the post-pandemic years as an exception before we returned to "normality"; we now have to accept that interest rates will be around 3.5% and that the U.S. Treasury yield will keep yielding around 4%. This is a new risk-free baseline. It neither kills the Mag 7 nor the rest of the market, but it implies that stocks have to justify their valuation accordingly to the fact that risk-averse investors can earn almost 4% on cash. Moreover, most of the Mag 7 have super balance sheets while also being cash flow generating machines. As a result, they don't need to rely on debt, and this is why only a few weeks ago, Amazon issued its first U.S. bond in three years. For the S&P 493, yields are somewhat of a filter. Companies with high levels of debt have suffered as they have seen their interest expense move up. This is the cost of overleveraging. Companies with weak pricing power have also suffered, especially in the categories of consumer discretionary and food. At the same time, companies with high earning power that are able to generate steady and growing cash flows can become more and more attractive as this high-yield environment continues.

In other words, 2026 should be a year where narratives will play a shrinking role compared to reality. Valuations will reflect this.

Pillar 2: AI Phase 2

As soon as ChatGPT was released in late 2022, the AI capex cycle began, and it has taken most of us aback as we have observed the big hyperscalers easily deploy $200B-$300B a year almost completely through self-funding. This investment cycle won't stop just because we change calendar years. But after three years of investments, AI is starting to permeate the enterprise world, and investors are shifting their attention toward AI ROI. Alphabet alone told us during the last earnings call that almost half of its code during Q3 was written through AI. Amazon is integrating AI into its warehouses, where robotics and logistics are intertwined. I have already told my readers that I know several fast fashion companies that are using AI to spot new trends two years in advance (and they get it right!). AI phase 2 doesn't mean we should move out of the hyperscalers, but that the earnings tree becomes wider as the benefits of AI spread across the economy.

Now, although it may be too early to say, if we look below, we see that this year, US Productivity has rapidly increased and, according to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, increased 3.3% in Q2, which is a 1.5% YoY increase. This is happening while industrial production is barely positive and has increased its productivity "only" by 2.5%. I expect this trend to continue and to become more evident in 2026. If this is so, we will see more and more companies report a better input/output ratio, aka, higher margins. Guess who could benefit first? A category that has been shunned so far: software players such as Salesforce (CRM) and Adobe (ADBE), of which I have recently written that it is "the" opportunity, awarding a strong buy rating.

Pillar 3: Manufacturing

We should now overlook the material aspect of the revolution. We are seeing infrastructure being built all around the country and the globe. We also know that the U.S. has made the Manufacturing Renaissance one of its top priorities. We are seeing reshoring policies, of which tariffs play an important role. But we are also seeing the government take stakes in public companies such as Intel or mandate what technologies can or can't be exported. Nonetheless, manufacturing activity is still stagnant. If we look at the total index, we see that industrial production is at around 101.43 (index 2017=100). But this index covers manufacturing, mining, and utilities. In case of an energy crisis or bad weather, this index can therefore grow without reflecting true expansion. If we erase "the noise" of weather and commodity patterns and want to focus only on factory production, we can use the index below, which better reflects the manufacturing sector. Here, we are below 2017 levels, with a print of 97.77. As we can see, since 2019, we have been steadily below 100.

Now, while the industrial production index tells us what happened, we can use another index that can help us understand what is going to happen: the ISM manufacturing PMI. This is based on sentiment and orders, instead of being built on current output. In other words, it is a leading indicator and tells us how many orders purchasing managers are signing. Although this index seems to have bottomed in 2023, it is having a hard time crawling back to above 50, which would clearly signal expansion mode.

The truth is that manufacturing still isn't booming, but the opposite is also true: it is not contracting more than it did in the past. Inventory cycles are also improving in many areas, although we have read that heavy-machinery manufacturers such as John Deere (DE) are still seeing their inventories pile up. A catalyst I expect to appear in 2026 is the ISM Manufacturing PMI coming closer to 50 and staying in the area for months. If this new level holds, it becomes more plausible for the S&P 493 to accelerate its EPS growth. In this case, keep an eye on Class 1 railroads such as Canadian National (CNI) or CSX Corporation (CSX).

Pillar 4: Monetization

Monetization is not an issue that AI stocks face. It is something the whole economy deals with. Now, let's focus on the past two pillars, which talked about Phase 2 and manufacturing activity. If we move in the direction of construction spending, which has been greatly affected by the current AI buildout, we see that we are $1.1T above pre-pandemic levels. Since we know the housing market is struggling, we can rightly guess that most of this boost comes from reshoring, factories, data centers, grid expansion and enhancement projects, logistics, and similar. When we look below, we see that public spending has almost doubled, moving from around $300B to almost $600B, while private spending has increased by around 60% from $1T to $1.6T. This is consistent with our reading of these numbers.

We should now think that a new construction boom is underway: spending has been high but stable for almost two years. However, we can expect harvesting to start as big funds were already being invested in it two or three years ago.

Who is going to benefit from the new infrastructure? I think the clearest winners will be the electrical equipment industry and utilities. This is why I am long Prysmian (PRYMY), and I think of companies such as Enbridge (ENB) or ONEOK (OKE), of which I wrote that "as AI builds, ONEOK bills", to give you the idea.

Sectors such as the ones mentioned above are big constituents of the S&P 493, and we are seeing the early signs of newly materialized profits. If we want to say this with a corporate joke, we could state that 2026 ends the time of rewarding nice PowerPoint presentations and replaces them with those who actually build and profit from capex investments.

Pillar 5: Energy Prices

I said right away that my 2026 includes lower crude prices. This means that industrials, transportation companies, and even consumer companies will benefit from a margin tailwind. Utilities and energy stocks should not be hurt because rising demand for power (electricity, in particular) will more than offset low commodity prices. So, energy producers and even midstream companies should operate well. In fact, what they need the most is a stable pricing environment. At these levels, they all make profits (breakevens are usually below $45 per barrel), while most consumers also benefit and can therefore feel lower inflationary pressure. This could help improve consumer sentiment, which we know is also key to consumer spending. If consumer sentiment improves, most of the S&P 493 companies will have an easier time reporting higher growth, also thanks to easy comparables versus 2025. In particular, companies with the highest operating leverage (industrial and transportation, for example) see the quickest EPS expansion when rates are stable and energy is cheap.

Pillar 6: Demographics

This is the most overlooked, yet it could have a major impact on the economy. Labor scarcity is caused not only by a lack of talent or by people unwilling to go to work. The population in Western economies is aging, and in recent years, it has been able to expand only through immigration policies, which have sparked a lot of debate. This is favorable for the unemployment rate, but it should also imply that wage floors will hold up better than in the past. Even more importantly, automation and autonomous solutions will be a big structural trend that many companies will pursue. Raise your hand if you expect ten years from now to keep seeing Amazon deliver a box as it is doing today with its fleet of drivers. The automation that we will all see in a decade has already started, and 2026 will speed up this process.

The Bull Case For Breadth

Let's quickly recap what we have seen. I expect more valuation discipline as a consequence of higher yields. I expect AI to be deployed and to increase productivity across many sectors. Manufacturing should start rebounding after a three-year recession. Capex will start being monetized, and energy prices will be a tailwind.

In this environment, the Mag 7 - in particular, the hyperscalers - keep winning, but the victory extends. They won't be winning alone.

Let's put together the data and draw a valuation frame. Pillar 1 leads us to realistic valuations. The other pillars support that EPS growth should improve next year, mainly because of the broadening thesis.

Currently, the fwd P/E ratio for the S&P 500 is 22.4, which is above the 5-yr and the 10-yr averages of 20 and 18.7, respectively. However, we should also note that in the last three months, while the fwd EPS estimate has moved up by 4%, the index has only moved up by 2.5%, which means that the multiple has slightly compressed (it was 22.8 at the end of September). The two highest ratios are found in Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology. They both stand close to 28.5, but for two different reasons.
